description: The open back nasal — 'dans', 'temps', 'chambre', 'enfant'. Drop your jaw as for 'ah' and let the air resonate through the nose; never pronounce the n or m itself. English speakers tend to close the vowel and add a consonant ('dahn') — the n must dissolve into the vowel.

minimalPairs.note: nasal 'bench' vs oral 'low' — same mouth, nose on/off

description: The rounded nasal — 'bon', 'non', 'nom', 'maison'. Round your lips as for 'oh' and send the air through the nose. Critically distinct from [ɑ̃]: 'blanc' (white) vs 'blond' (blond), 'lent' (slow) vs 'long' (long) differ only by this contrast.

minimalPairs.note: [ɑ̃] vs [ɔ̃] — jaw open vs lips rounded

description: The front nasal — 'vin', 'pain', 'plein', 'matin', 'bien'. Smile slightly as for 'eh' and nasalize. In modern Metropolitan French the historical fourth nasal [œ̃] ('un', 'brun') has merged into [ɛ̃] for most speakers — so 'brin' and 'brun' sound identical in Paris. You may still hear the distinction in the South and Belgium.

The famous pair. French 'u' [y] does not exist in English: say 'ee' and round your lips into a tight circle without moving your tongue. 'ou' [u] is the familiar 'oo'. The stakes are real: 'tu' (you) vs 'tout' (all), 'rue' (street) vs 'roue' (wheel), 'dessus' (above) vs 'dessous' (below) — and the classic danger pair 'cul' (ass) vs 'cou' (neck).

minimalPairs.note: 'above' vs 'below' — opposite meanings, one vowel apart

description: The French r is made at the back of the throat — a soft gargle against the uvula, not the English tongue-tip r and not a Spanish trill. Practice trick: hold a 'g' position and breathe out a gentle friction, as in a very light clearing of the throat: 'rue', 'Paris', 'merci'. At word ends it's softer: 'bonjour', 'partir'. An English r is the single strongest marker of an anglophone accent — but it never blocks comprehension, so improve it without fear.

minimalPairs.note: uvular r vs plain l onset

description: Most final consonants are silent: peti(t), gran(d), tro(p), françai(s), Pari(s). The memorable exceptions: final C, R, F, L are usually pronounced — the 'CaReFuL' consonants: sac, bonjour, neuf, hôtel. (Even this has exceptions: 'er' endings are [e] — parler, boulanger.) A silent final consonant is not dead, just dormant: it wakes in the feminine (petite) and in liaison (petit ami [pə.ti.t‿a.mi]).

minimalPairs.note: silent -t wakes up in the feminine

description: A normally-silent final consonant is pronounced when the next word starts with a vowel, gluing the words: vous‿avez [vu.z‿a.ve], les‿amis [le.z‿a.mi], un petit‿ami. Liaison s and x sound as [z]; d as [t] (grand‿homme [gʁɑ̃.t‿ɔm]). Mandatory after articles, pronouns and short prepositions (les_amis, en_été); forbidden after 'et' and before h aspiré (les | héros). When in doubt, make the common ones and skip the rest — over-liaison sounds stranger than under-liaison.

minimalPairs.note: liaison appears only before a vowel

description: The unaccented e is the disappearing vowel of French. At word ends it's silent — 'porte' is one syllable [pɔʁt] — its real job being to make the consonant before it audible. Mid-word and in little words (je, ne, le, ce) fast speech deletes it: 'samedi' → [sam.di], 'je ne sais pas' → 'j'sais pas' [ʃɛ.pa]. When pronounced, it's the neutral schwa [ə] of 'le'. Understanding e-deletion is half of understanding fast spoken French.

minimalPairs.note: final e silent vs final é fully voiced

description: é (accent aigu) is closed [e] — lips slightly spread, like the vowel in 'café': été, parlé, marché. è and ê are open [ɛ], like 'bed': père, très, fête, même. The pair 'j'ai parlé' [e] vs 'je parlais' [ɛ] distinguishes passé composé from imparfait by this vowel alone in careful speech. Endings -er and -ez are [e]; -et and -ais/-ait are [ɛ].

Two rounded front vowels English lacks. Closed [ø] in open syllables: 'deux', 'peu', 'bleu' — say é and round your lips. Open [œ] before a pronounced consonant: 'sœur', 'heure', 'beurre' — say è and round. Rule of thumb: syllable ends in the vowel → closed; syllable ends in a consonant → open. Don't substitute English 'oo' or 'uh' — 'deux' (two) must not collapse into 'douze' (twelve) territory.

Always the glide [wa], never 'oy': 'moi' [mwa], 'toi', 'trois', 'voiture', 'boire'. English speakers see 'oi' and produce the vowel of 'boy' — retrain the reflex: oi = 'wah'. With a circumflex or in 'oin' it changes: 'loin' is [lwɛ̃] (w + nasal).
The palatal nasal, like the 'ny' in 'canyon': 'montagne' [mɔ̃.taɲ], 'gagner', 'champagne', 'magnifique'. Never a hard g + n sequence. Say 'ni' of 'onion' with the tongue flat against the palate.

minimalPairs.note: one palatal sound, not two consonants

description: After a vowel, -ill- and final -il are the glide [j] (English 'y'): 'fille' [fij], 'travail' [tʁa.vaj], 'soleil' [sɔ.lɛj], 'bouteille'. The trap: a handful of common words keep [l] — 'ville' [vil], 'mille' [mil], 'tranquille' [tʁɑ̃.kil]. So 'fille' and 'ville' do NOT rhyme, the single most reliable way to spot a learner reading aloud.

minimalPairs.note: the classic exception pair — girl vs city
both end [aj] — noun and verb sound alike

description: c is hard [k] before a, o, u ('café', 'cou') and soft [s] before e, i ('cinéma', 'centre'). The cedilla ç forces [s] where the following vowel would make it hard: 'ça', 'français', 'garçon', 'reçu'. So ç never appears before e or i — it wouldn't be needed.

g is hard [g] before a, o, u ('gare', 'goût') and soft [ʒ] — the 's' of 'measure' — before e, i ('genre', 'gilet', 'manger'). To force softness before a/o, French inserts a silent e: 'mangeons' [mɑ̃.ʒɔ̃], 'voyageons'. To force hardness before e/i, it inserts u: 'guerre' [gɛʁ], 'guide' [gid]. French [ʒ] never has an English 'j' [dʒ] onset — 'je' is [ʒə], not 'juh'.

qu is plain [k], never [kw]: 'qui' [ki], 'que' [kə], 'quatre' [katʁ], 'question' [kɛs.tjɔ̃], 'quartier'. The anglophone reflex 'kwestion', 'kwartier' is instantly recognizable — suppress the w entirely. (Rare exceptions in learned words: 'aquarium' [a.kwa.ʁjɔm].)

The letter h is never pronounced in French — but it comes in two grammatical flavors. H muet behaves like a vowel: elision and liaison apply — l'homme, l'hôtel, les‿hommes [le.z‿ɔm]. H aspiré blocks them: le héros (never 'l'héros' — that would sound like 'les zéros', the zeros!), la honte, le haricot, les | haricots without liaison. There's no rule for which is which; dictionaries mark aspiré with *. Learn the common aspiré words as exceptions.

A single s between vowels is voiced [z]: 'maison' [mɛ.zɔ̃], 'chose', 'visiter'. Double ss stays [s]: 'boisson', 'poisson'. The high-stakes pair: 'poisson' (fish, [s]) vs 'poison' (poison, [z]) — and 'dessert' [s] vs 'désert' [z]. When ordering dinner, the double s is your friend.

minimalPairs.note: [s] fish vs [z] poison — order the first one

description: French ch is [ʃ] as in 'shoe', never [tʃ] as in 'church': 'chat' [ʃa], 'chaud', 'chercher', 'chambre'. The English 'ch' reflex produces 'tchat' — shave off the t. (In Greek-derived words ch is [k]: 'chorale', 'psychologie', 'archéologie'.)

minimalPairs.note: the French word vs the borrowed 'online chat'

description: The ending -tion is [sjɔ̃] — 'attention' [a.tɑ̃.sjɔ̃], 'question' [kɛs.tjɔ̃], 'addition', 'réservation'. Never the English 'shun': no [ʃ], and the nasal [ɔ̃] replaces 'un'. Since English shares hundreds of -tion words with French, mastering this one ending instantly upgrades a large chunk of vocabulary.

French has no lexical stress — no syllable inside a word is punched the way English does ('PHO-to-graph'). Instead, every syllable gets even weight and the last syllable of a phrase gets a gentle lengthening: 'je vais au marché' rises slightly on -ché. Applying English stress patterns ('RES-taurant') is a bigger source of foreign accent than any single vowel. Think of French as a smooth stream of equal beats ending in a soft landing.
